annotations.text: The most compelling diagnosis, integrating the imaging, clinical course, and exclusion of systemic disease, is glioblastoma.

The two adjacent masses in the left occipital and posterior parietal regions, with heterogeneous enhancement, restricted diffusion, and transcallosal extension to the splenium, are highly characteristic of GBM.
The rapid progression from transient visual symptoms to persistent quadrantanopia suggests a malignant infiltrative process with evolving edema and mass effect.
The elevated LDH and stress hyperglycemia are consistent with a metabolically active tumor.
The presence of two adjacent masses raises the possibility of multifocal GBM, which occurs in approximately 5–10% of cases and is associated with a more aggressive course.
Heterogeneous enhancement is classic for GBM, reflecting areas of necrosis, viable tumor, and disrupted blood-brain barrier.
The absence of systemic metastases on imaging does not rule out GBM, as it is rarely metastatic outside the CNS.
